skill.name: twitter-automation
skill.description: Automate Twitter/X account actions through the inference.sh belt CLI, including posts, media posts, likes, retweets, direct messages, follows, profile lookup, and post deletion.
skill.summary: Run Twitter/X posting, engagement, messaging, and lookup workflows through belt CLI commands.
skill.author: halt-catch-fire

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified many shell command examples and external URLs, but the Ruby backtick and weak cryptography classifications are false positives from Markdown content. The remaining risk is moderate because the skill instructs users to run belt commands that can post, delete, like, retweet, follow, and send DMs through a live Twitter/X integration.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Commands Perform Live Social Account Actions
medium_findings.description: The skill documents belt commands for posting, liking, retweeting, sending direct messages, following users, deleting posts, and generating media for publication. These are intended capabilities, but they can change a connected Twitter/X account and should require user confirmation and valid account authorization.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples directly invoke belt app actions that create, delete, or modify Twitter/X content. The behavior is documented rather than hidden, so it is a legitimate but high-impact capability.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party Network Dependency for Twitter/X Integration
medium_findings.description: The skill relies on inference.sh documentation, hosted media, and belt app calls that contact external services. This is expected for the integration, but it means account data and post content may leave the local environment through that provider.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple cited lines reference inference.sh URLs and a media URL field. No credential theft is shown, but network use is central to the skill.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Hits Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak cryptography matches occur in descriptive Markdown/frontmatter and table text, not in executable cryptographic code. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar algorithms being used.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain the skill description and a Markdown table header. They do not contain code that implements or invokes cryptography.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Blocks Misclassified as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: The external command detector reported many Markdown code fences and inline command examples as Ruby or shell backtick execution. They are documentation examples, not code that executes automatically when the skill is loaded.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding Markdown clearly shows fenced bash examples and inline install commands. They still represent commands a user may run, but not hidden automatic execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Tool Access Limited to belt Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill declares Bash access for belt commands and provides examples that call belt app run and npx skills add. This enables useful automation but creates operational risk if prompts request unwanted account actions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and command examples are explicit. The risk depends on runtime user authorization and account connection state.

content.value_statement: Managing Twitter/X posts and engagement across tools can be slow and error-prone. This skill provides repeatable belt CLI workflows for posting, media publishing, engagement, messaging, and account lookup.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to authenticate with the belt CLI before using Twitter/X apps.
Provides commands to create text posts and media posts on Twitter/X.
Provides commands to like, retweet, follow users, and send direct messages.
Provides commands to retrieve user profiles and post details by username or ID.
Includes workflows for generating image or video assets before posting them.
Links to inference.sh documentation for Twitter/X integration setup.
limitations: Requires the belt CLI and an authorized inference.sh account integration.
Does not implement scheduling logic directly inside the skill file.
Does not verify Twitter/X API limits, account permissions, or policy compliance.
Can change a live social account if commands are run with valid credentials.

use_cases.title: Publish Campaign Updates
use_cases.description: Prepare and run repeatable commands for text and media posts during product or community campaigns.

use_cases.title: Test X Integration Flows
use_cases.description: Validate posting, profile lookup, and engagement actions while building against inference.sh Twitter/X apps.

use_cases.title: Create AI Media Posts
use_cases.description: Generate image or video assets through supported apps, then publish them with a Twitter/X post.

prompt_templates.title: Post a Simple Update
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me post this update to Twitter/X using the belt CLI: [draft text]. Ask for confirmation before running any command.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Media Post
prompt_templates.scenario: Post with an existing media URL
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare a Twitter/X media post with this text and media URL. Show the belt command and wait for approval before execution.

prompt_templates.title: Review an Engagement Action
prompt_templates.scenario: Like, retweet, follow, or direct message
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to [like, retweet, follow, or send a DM]. Check the required IDs, summarize the action, and ask before running belt.

prompt_templates.title: Build an AI Media Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a workflow that generates [image or video concept], captures the output media URL, and prepares a Twitter/X post command for review.

output_examples.input: User asks to post a short launch announcement.
output_examples.output: The assistant confirms the final post text, identifies that a text-post command is needed, and asks for approval before running it.

output_examples.input: User provides a tweet ID and asks to retweet it.
output_examples.output: The assistant verifies the tweet ID format, explains that the action will affect the connected account, and waits for confirmation.

output_examples.input: User asks to create an AI-generated image post.
output_examples.output: The assistant outlines the generation step, extracts the resulting media URL, prepares the post text, and requests approval before publishing.

best_practices: Confirm every posting, deletion, follow, retweet, like, and direct message before running a command.
Use test accounts or draft content when validating new automation workflows.
Review Twitter/X platform rules and organization approval requirements before automating engagement.
anti_patterns: Do not run account-changing commands without explicit user confirmation.
Do not automate spam, unsolicited direct messages, or deceptive engagement.
Do not paste private tokens, session secrets, or OAuth credentials into prompts.

faq.question: Does this skill post directly to Twitter/X?
faq.answer: It provides belt CLI commands that can post when the user has a configured and authorized integration.

faq.question: Does it require external services?
faq.answer: Yes. It relies on inference.sh, the belt CLI, and the connected Twitter/X account integration.

faq.question: Can it send direct messages?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes a direct message command, so users should confirm the recipient and text before execution.

faq.question: Can it delete posts?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples include post deletion by ID, which should be treated as a sensitive action.

faq.question: Does it include built-in rate limit handling?
faq.answer: No. Users must rely on the provider integration and Twitter/X platform limits.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish in the marketplace?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium risk rating because it performs live social account actions through external commands.
